sentences.text: A little bit, yes.
I tell you why.
Why-- in Buzuluk, under the Urals, most the Jewish immigrant, there was-- like I was have a this.
Not a officer or-- or officers from the army.
There were in my [? sergeants, ?] we have been nine.
Yes?
Nine people.
From the nine, were seven Jewish.
And from the seven Jewish, they must make officers.
So partly there were more Jewish officers than non-Jewish, than Czechs.
So all the top-- from the officers were mostly high percentage Jewish.
For example, in Britain, it was the Arab force.
There were no Jewish.
The army in Britain or in Middle East or in Tobruk, there wasn't Jewish.
There was 95% non-Jewish-- in Britain, in France, in Tobruk, and the Middle East, and Palestine exactly.
There were no Jewish.
Now in Russia, there was mostly Jewish [INAUDIBLE] fly from Czechoslovakia to the East.
It was too late to come to Palestine or to-- to-- Britain.
So mostly Jewish in the East front.
So partly the Czechs a little bit anti-Semitic.
No.
No.
One thing-- it was not in my-- in the tanks, in the infantry.
That was a Jewish-- I don't know-- something he make-- he-- he was-- he was-- I don't know how to say in English.
He make the food for the army.
And it's true or not-- they told him he sell the food to the local people.
So [? the-- it ?] goes to the-- and [? he ?] [? shot ?] him.
It is the one what I remember.
No.
There was actually not a anti-Semitic thing.
It was he sell the food from the army to the civilian.
Is true or not.
It was in a different part of the Czechoslovakia.
And in the tanks, no.
It wasn't.
Was a few [? this-- ?] my commander have three adjutants.
Yes?
From the three, was two Jewish.
I and the other one.
And one not Jewish.
What was the other man's name?
Do you remember?
I don't remember.
Hiram!
Was his name.
He-- he stayed in the army.
He died.
Last year, I was in Prague with [INAUDIBLE] in Prague too, the [?
Czechoslovakian ?] [? agent ?], [? as ?] [?
I ?] [? started ?] so.
From these hundreds these, I have seen a few people.
And with-- he knows we was in the [? tank ?] [? center. ?] I must ask him, where are you?
Or ?] [? the ?] [? girls? ?] [?
Or ?] [? girls? ?] There's 70 years, 75 years [INAUDIBLE] [? girls. ?] Where are you?
We haven't seen each other over 50 years.
So always where are you?
Where you have been?
And after, oh!
You remember this.
You said in the army you went--
Yes.
No.
The camps?
In this time, it was a house.
It was--
Huh?
A house.
It was a few days through-- [? ours ?] it was liberated.
There was the Soviet.
Yes?
They liberated, the Soviet army.
They don't know what to do with the people.
You know?
There wasn't Soviet people.
There was all mostly from central Europe people and mostly old or ill people.
So the-- partly, they take it to Russia.
Now, most of the people don't like go to the Russia.
So again, they run away back to Poland or to Germany or Czechoslovakia or Hungary-- some individuals.
Yes, I try.
I ask.
The-- most of the question was there food.
They were always hungry.
Oh, yes.
I-- my wife and I, we take from the army.
I asked.
They give us mostly bread, bread and some salamis and like this.
Though it was, again, difficult even though the doctor was there.
They told, don't give them eat.
They are hungry for months and months.
They can't eat this.
It is worse that when they eat a half a bread, they will be ill and die.
Again, difficulties [? what ?] they eating.
What?
I hope.
No, I don't find nobody.
So I lost in Auschwitz three brothers, from my oldest brother, three children, and from all the family-- from the aunts and this-- I lost 47 people.
Now, from my family, only seven.
Yes.
Few people.
I-- and helped them to go through the [?
Karpacz ?] to-- back to Jelenia from Poland.
No.
It was-- for me, to run out [INAUDIBLE].
It was finished with what my wife said about the chimneys.
So when I don't find them, I-- first, second day, I must go back to the army.
I was the commander.
So I [? can't ?] stay there.
And secondly, it wasn't nothing to do there.
It was-- I can't tell you the feeling.
To see people like skeletons.
What you can say?
They wasn't interested.
They was interested only-- only something to eat.
About the anti-Semitism-- was when we stay, it was not far from Auschwitz.
They come-- I was the-- the commandant in this-- part of this-- comes to [INAUDIBLE].
And the boy was asked-- was [? on the ?] charge.
He ask to come-- come into the commandant.
And the boy-- it was a boy from Carpatho, Russia, a Czech soldier.
[INAUDIBLE] yes, the commandant is here.
He's a Jewish?
No.
He's a very nice boy.
It was about me.
So they come-- he let them in.
So the two, they was from Jelenia.
I don't remember now.
They was in the camp.
Where they ?] come in the camp in the last transport.
So there was more or less feet to walk.
So my wife, they give them a meal-- eat.
And we have on there-- I remember-- on the table, sugar cubes.
And we sit, and they always take the sugar.
So my wife told him, I have enough sugar.
It's no problem.
Now, I don't think it is good to your empty stomach to eat so sugar.
I give.
You take it with you.
So it's better that you stop.
It is like-- so stories about that come back.
Have you any other stories you want to tell us like that?
Other stories?
After the war, I started in Jelenia in the business from my father-- it belongs to my brother-- up to 1948.
And in 1948, it started-- [? the ?] new-- how you say-- regime-- the communist run the-- this.
So it was the Cold War Era.
So I don't want to stay in Jelenia.
And my wife, before the war, she works in Prague.
So she want to go to Prague.
So I manage it to come to Prague.
How did you get to Prague?
No.
I-- I had the permission.
So I go.
So I go with the two children.
I-- through the army, I-- I was-- made a change.
One move from Prague to Ostrow, to Ostrow to some town in Slovakia, there to Jelenia.
So it was four people to change.
Now, this-- in this time, the flats belong to the army.
So I got a nice flat in Prague-- again, same, and communist.
Again, my colleague from the army asked my-- the man, he managed the house-- what you say about your new tenants?
[INAUDIBLE], oh, he's a nice boy.
No. ?] She's a Jewish.
It was the answer about the anti-Semitism.
The other anti-Semitism was [? worse. ?]
I work in the-- in different places up and down, up and down.
And one time, I worked in a translator to a Soviet advisor.
All on the country was Soviet advisor.
So I work with him, translate and so on.
And what happens always-- I was up and down, up and down.
Why? ?] I had a brother in Israel.
He go away in 1933 to Palestine.
He married.
And he died five years.
And my sister was in Britain.
So there are always-- you can work in [?
Soviet ?] position.
You have relatives in the West.
So I must again to down, some work [? up. ?] So once I go to the top to the ministry [? say, ?] look.
You always find out when you don't need me.
Yes?
Yes, you find something.
When you must push me out from a nice job, I have relatives in the West.
[INAUDIBLE].
My brother-- I was a young boy-- he leave Czechoslovakian in 1933.
He don't ask the mother.
He don't ask the father.
He goes to Palestine.
Yes?
He was not a youngster.
My sister, she was married.
She goes with her husband to-- to Britain.
She don't ask, again, the father, the mother [? what died. ?] Yes?
And she don't ask me.
She goes with her husband.
So why I am responsible for [? the-- ?] Yes, it is you are-- yes.
And secondly, I am not in contact with them.
So what-- what is wrong always with me?
I am up, down.
When you need me, you take me out.
When you don't need me, you push me out.
So I stay and work [? around ?] around up to 1968.
Let me just interrupt you a second.
What?
No.
My father luckily, luckily died three weeks before the transport in bed, in Jelenia.
I must-- interesting thing-- my sister--
My sister, the older sister.
In Jelenia.
In Britain.
Oh.
In Jelenia?
Yes.
Jelenia.
She was in the camp waiting for transport to-- to Auschwitz.
And one from the commandants was my friend before the war.
We go together to the school, and we was good friends.
And he knows the family.
So [? she ?] come to my sister in the evening, and he give her a permission for 24 hours to leave the camp and after 24 hours to come back.
And he told her, go out and disappear from Jelenia.
Don't come back.
So my sister goes out.
And the first train, she goes to Presov.
In the morning, they took all the people in the train to Auschwitz.
So she stay [INAUDIBLE] alive.
And you mentioned you had-- when did you have your first daughter?
My first daughter, I have only in 1947.
While after the war, my wife, she was ill.
So '45, '46, she come be pregnant.
One daughter in-- April-- no.
In May '47.
And the second daughter in 10 and 1/2 months later in '48.
More or less, yes.
So finished [? at ?] Prague.
In 1968, both the [? daughter ?] have been under university, and they come to Britain.
Them ?] [? opera ?] girls.
Work, stay by sister, and work in [? opera girls. ?] In 1968, August, the Soviets liberated Czechoslovakia the second time.
It was the Prague Spring.
So again, [INAUDIBLE] Prague was full with Soviets and German army from the East [? this. ?] So it was a big question-- what do?
The daughters come back or I go-- we go to emigrate to this.
My wife, she [? wasn't-- ?] was ill.
She had something with the-- this and [INAUDIBLE].
I said, I don't want the children come back to Prague.
So I manage it via my sister to got the visa to Britain.
Listen.
I was 58 years old, or 58 years young.
I started with my ill wife, a new life.
I don't spoke three words in English.
So I come to Britain, thanks to my sister and late brother-in-law.
They help me very much.
First of all, he give me a place to stay, to live.
I have here a good friend, and he have some contacts.
And he found me a job in Courtaulds, these textile, Anglo-Canadian company, one of the biggest in the world.
So I was there for a appointment.
I had a translator.
And say the translator, via translator, please tell him I don't speak English.
So-- and the director who make the appointment said, tell him we don't pay for talking.
We pay you for working.
And for your work, you don't need the English.
It is why my English is so bad after so many years.
Why?
I work with digits only-- planning, analysis, and this one.
I learned very quickly to say 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and so on.
When somebody from the head office ask me some question, I answer in figures.
I say, 25, 23, 155, and so on.
No.
I don't talk about the weather.
So I worked there in Courtaulds, yes.
What was interesting, the [INAUDIBLE] [? economist, ?] they was very, very nice people.
They took me with 58 years in the private pension scheme.
You can imagine that I was not a bad worker when they take me.
So when I was 65, the unions don't agree to stay.
So they told me, you can stay, [? tell ?] [? me, ?] part-time.
It is difficult.
It's too far to come to [? once ?] [INAUDIBLE] from [INAUDIBLE].
So I found [? a ?] other job in Piccadilly, you know.
They're ?] same in the textile.
So there I work after my pension from this.
So I work up to my 70.
I stopped when I was 70 years old.
Let me take you back to Prague first.
Yes.
Yes.
In Prague, I go to the evening university.
Yes?
I told you I have the [? diploma ?] [INAUDIBLE] economist after I study four years.
No.
In a flat.
Yeah.
A very nice flat.
It-- it belonged to the army.
It is what I got it [INAUDIBLE] was in the army.
So in between, come back to the army.
So I finished the war.
I'm ?] captain.
Yes?
After the 10 years of this, they give me a higher rank.
Then ?] [? a ?] major.
And again, after I-- 20 years jubilee from the war, they gave me the other this.
They ?] [? make ?] [? a ?] colonel lieutenant.
Yes?
And in the '90s, they took me from a colonel.
So I am a big for nothing.
So in London, I work--
It was-- when the Russian come in, it was, again, the strong communist regime.
Always.
Again, the Dubcek was out, the Prague Spring, and again, the strong communist regime.
The-- the Communist Party was on the top.
Theoretically, was the minister and some of this [INAUDIBLE].
The country was under the hand and command from the communists.
What?
I was-- difficulties.
Always [? on ?] the university director, he was same in the army.
It was difficult for me to take my children in the university.
He was [? director, ?] and we have been together in the army in the war time.
So [? they ?] help me, what was a very unique thing.
Both my daughters started study at the university.
First off, them [? a ?] Jewish.
Secondly, from a-- not a [? proletariat ?] father.
So the older daughter-- she will be here-- she started study medicine.
And the younger daughter, she started study language and linguistics.
I don't want.
It was-- [? why ?] I have seen why they come back from a free country in the West to a dictatorship, [? the ?] communist and this.
And secondly, my sister don't want-- let them back.
